description:
You are the release-manager bot.  
  Your job is to prepare environment-variable files and an export log that the rest of the DevOps pipeline will later consume.

  Initial project layout (already present):
    /home/user/deployments/                    – directory is empty when you begin
    /home/user/.bashrc                         – standard bashrc already exists; you must append to it, not replace it.

  Goal – after you finish, the file-system must look exactly like this (indentation shows hierarchy):
    /home/user/deployments/
        envs/
            staging.env
            production.env
        current.env         -> symbolic link that must point to either staging.env or production.env
        logs/
            export_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log   (see exact timestamp format rule below)

  1. Create the directory /home/user/deployments/envs and place TWO dotenv files in it:

     1.1  /home/user/deployments/envs/staging.env
          Exact required contents (5 lines, no leading/trailing whitespace):
              APP_ENV=staging
              API_URL=https://staging.api.example.com
              DB_HOST=staging-db.internal
              DB_PORT=5432
              RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15

     1.2  /home/user/deployments/envs/production.env
          Exact required contents (5 lines, no leading/trailing whitespace):
              APP_ENV=production
              API_URL=https://api.example.com
              DB_HOST=prod-db.internal
              DB_PORT=5432
              RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15

  2. Choose “staging” as the active environment for now.  
     Implement this by creating a symbolic link named /home/user/deployments/current.env that points to /home/user/deployments/envs/staging.env  
     The link’s pathname target **must** be a relative path (“envs/staging.env”), not an absolute one.

  3. Persist the activation for all future shells:

     • Append the single line
         source /home/user/deployments/current.env
       to the end of /home/user/.bashrc
       – Append only once; if you re-run your script the line must not be duplicated.

  4. Generate an export log in /home/user/deployments/logs.  

     4.1  The log file name must follow the pattern:
           export_YYYYMMDD_HHMMSS.log
          where the timestamp corresponds to the local system time *at the moment you create the file*.

     4.2  The file must begin with a header line in this exact format (hashes, spaces, and colon included):
           ### Environment Export Log: YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS ###

          Use the same timestamp that you used in the filename but reformatted with hyphens and a space.

     4.3  After the header, list all key-value pairs that were just sourced from current.env.
          • One VARIABLE=VALUE per line.
          • The list must be sorted alphabetically by VARIABLE name.
          • End the file with a single trailing newline character.

     A correct example *shape* for staging (values must obviously match your file’s content):

         ### Environment Export Log: 2023-09-15 15:42:07 ###
         API_URL=https://staging.api.example.com
         APP_ENV=staging
         DB_HOST=staging-db.internal
         DB_PORT=5432
         RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15

  5. Immediately apply the new environment to the current shell so that a command like
         echo $APP_ENV
     outputs “staging” after your script finishes.

  Important verification notes (follow them precisely – the automated grader is strict):

    • Filenames, directory names and link names are case-sensitive.
    • Do NOT leave additional blank lines in any dotenv or log file.
    • The symbolic link must use a relative path and must point to the exact file specified.
    • The timestamp inside the header line must exactly match the one in the filename except for the formatting rules stated above.
    • The export log must exist inside /home/user/deployments/logs.
    • Do not hard-code the date/time; generate it programmatically.
    • Ensure you do not require sudo or root privileges.
    • You may create intermediate scripts, but they must live inside the user’s home (e.g., /home/user). 
    • The automated test will source /home/user/.bashrc in a fresh shell and expect the environment variables from /home/user/deployments/current.env to be available.

  Produce no extraneous output. It is fine for commands to emit their usual stdout/stderr, but do not create additional files outside those described above.
truth:
Expected final artefacts (exact byte-for-byte contents):

  1. /home/user/deployments/envs/staging.env
         APP_ENV=staging
         API_URL=https://staging.api.example.com
         DB_HOST=staging-db.internal
         DB_PORT=5432
         RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15\n   ← single trailing LF (0x0A)

  2. /home/user/deployments/envs/production.env
         APP_ENV=production
         API_URL=https://api.example.com
         DB_HOST=prod-db.internal
         DB_PORT=5432
         RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15\n

  3. /home/user/deployments/current.env
         symbolic link whose link target is literally “envs/staging.env” (11 bytes)

  4. /home/user/.bashrc
       The original .bashrc lines (unspecified) followed by exactly ONE extra line (with a preceding LF if needed):
         source /home/user/deployments/current.env

  5. /home/user/deployments/logs/export_????????_??????.log  
       The filename timestamp will be captured at runtime.  
       Content pattern (using the same timestamp T = YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS derived from the filename):

         ### Environment Export Log: {T} ###
         API_URL=https://staging.api.example.com
         APP_ENV=staging
         DB_HOST=staging-db.internal
         DB_PORT=5432
         RELEASE_VERSION=2023.09.15\n

  The test harness will:

    • Read the link target of /home/user/deployments/current.env and expect “envs/staging.env”.
    • Source /home/user/.bashrc in a new bash process and expect:
          $APP_ENV == "staging"
          $API_URL == "https://staging.api.example.com"
          etc.
    • Verify that the export log exists, the header timestamp matches the filename timestamp, and the key-value lines match the contents of staging.env in alphabetical order.
name: task_000000_b0f2206c
